Conseil du ministre
REASONS FOR DECISION
OVERVIEW
[1] XXXX XXXX (Principal Appellant) and XXXX XXXX (Associate Appellant), citizens of Hungary, appealed a decision of the Refugee Protection Division (RPD), dated February 25, 2019, rejecting their claims for refugee protection.
DETERMINATION
[2] Pursuant to Section 111(1)(b) of the Immigration and Refugee Protection Act (IRPA),1 the Refugee Appeal Division (RAD) sets aside the determination of the RPD and substitutes its determination that the Appellants are Convention refugees.
BACKGROUND
[3] The Appellants alleged before the RPD that they experienced discrimination and racism in Hungary because of their Roma ethnicity. They alleged they were evicted from their home, physically attacked, and that they experienced discrimination in employment, healthcare, and in their daily lives, without any meaningful assistance from the police.
[4] The Appellants' applications for refugee protection were heard on XXXX XXXX, 2019. By a decision of February 25, 2019, the RPD rejected their claims. The RPD found that the Appellants lacked credibility, that the effect of mistreatment suffered by the Appellants did not rise to persecution, and that state protection would be reasonably forthcoming to them if they experienced problems on return to Hungary.
[5] The Appellants argue that the RPD erred in its assessment of the Appellants' credibility, by ignoring or misconstruing evidence, by applying the incorrect legal test for persecution, and in its analysis of state protection.
ROLE OF THE RAD
[6] The RAD reviews decisions of the RPD on a standard of correctness after conducting an independent assessment of the evidence before it.2 The exception to this rule is where the RPD enjoys a meaningful advantage in the assessment and weighing of oral testimony heard, which, in particular cases, may attract deference. Unless specifically stated below, I have applied a correctness standard.
ANALYSIS
[7] I find that the determinative issues in this appeal are whether the Appellants would face discrimination amounting to persecution should they return to Hungary and whether state protection would be available to them.
The RPD's Findings
[8] The RPD considered that the Principal Appellant was an unsophisticated witness and found that the Appellants lived a difficult life in Hungary, but did not find that the credible discrimination relayed amounted to persecution. The RPD found that the Associate Appellant's elbow injury was not due to an assault by skinheads because this was not mentioned at the port-of-entry (POE), the medical report about the elbow does not make reference to how the injury occurred, the police reporting portion at the bottom of the medical report is blank, no other police report was requested, and the Principal Appellant exaggerated his testimony by testifying that an ambulance was called and the doctor within the ambulance insulted them. The RPD also found the Principal Appellant fabricated that skinheads marched in his neighbourhood, threw Molotov cocktails, and the police did not help, as it was not mentioned at the POE, the Principal Appellant's testimony was inconsistent, and it was not widely reported in the objective evidence.
[9] The RPD also found the Appellants did not face discrimination with respect to their housing as they did not establish, on a balance of probabilities, that they were living in the area affected by the demolition or that they were evicted.
[10] The RPD also found there had been no denial of medical care for the Appellants in Hungary.
[11] Overall, the RPD found that discrimination amounting to persecution was not established, and that state protection would be reasonably forthcoming if the Appellants experienced problems on return to Hungary.
The Appellants' Arguments
[12] The Appellants argue that the RPD erred in its assessment of the Appellants' credibility by failing to account for their educational unsophistication, being overly microscopic and erroneous, particularly regarding the POE notes, supporting documents, and objective evidence.
[13] With regard to the omission of the assault and throwing of cocktails from the POE notes, they argue that this was not irregular when considering the explanation that the Principal Appellant was nervous and excited, and when taking into account his lack of education and that he was deemed an unsophisticated witness.
[14] With regard to the Associate Appellant's elbow injury, they submit that the medical reports were corroborative evidence and no issue was raised as to their authenticity. A lack of additional corroborative evidence was not a valid basis to impugn the Appellants' credibility and it was unreasonable to expect the Appellants to have obtained a police report considering the objective evidence about the lack of assistance and discrimination by police. From paragraph 20 of the Appellants' Memorandum, they point to several sections of the National Documentation Package (NDP) to highlight the lack of adequate state protection which was ignored by the RPD.
[15] The Appellants further argue the RPD ignored or misconstrued evidence with regard to their housing and social assistance as the medical evidence showed they lived at an address in the same neighbourhood and area of XXXX as the numbered streets. Further, they argue the RPD ignored that the Appellants lived with family members from 2015-2017, which explains how they received social assistance, and that the RPD contradicted itself which led to an incorrect finding of fact. They further submit that there is objective evidence that corroborates they did not receive money or an offer of relocation when evicted. They further submit that the RPD wrongly took issue with the support letter from the Vice Chair of the Roma Self-government in XXXX as it came from a reputable source and its authenticity was not found to be an issue.
[16] The Appellants also argue the RPD ignored and misconstrued the objective evidence, failed to consider evidence of similarly situation individuals, applied the wrong legal test for persecution by failing to consider cumulative discrimination amounting to persecution, and erred in its analysis of state protection.
The RAD's Assessment
[17] I have reviewed the record and conducted an independent assessment of the evidence. I find the Appellants' arguments regarding the RPD's errors and the cumulative effect of the discrimination they experienced in Hungary have merit.
[18] I agree with the Appellants that the RPD failed to account for their educational unsophistication when assessing the POE notes and that the RPD was microscopic when assessing their supporting documents.
[19] The RPD took issue with the Appellants' omission of the assault and throwing of Molotov cocktails from their first POE interview, as well as the omission of the throwing of Molotov cocktails from their second interview. The RPD noted that the Appellants have family members in Canada and that the Appellants possessed information on the refugee process prior to coming to Canada. The Appellants argue that they cannot be expected to provide all details of their claim at the POE, as stated by the Federal Court. They argue that this, combined with the Appellants' unsophistication and explanation that he was nervous and excited, explain why it is not irregular for information to be lacking in the POE notes. I agree with the Appellants. The Appellants are unsophisticated and were unrepresented at the time of the POE interview. Therefore, what can be reasonably expected from their answers is less than what would be reasonably expected from someone more sophisticated or who had the benefit of counsel. The Appellants provided a short and general explanation of what they fear if they returned to Hungary and no follow up questions asked of them. I do not find that the omission of the assault and throwing of Molotov cocktails from either interview was significant as the Appellants reasonably would have known that they would have the opportunity to provide more details in their Basis of Claim forms and at the hearing, based on the experiences of their family members in Canada. Further, details about the assault provided at the second interview were more serious than the throwing of Molotov cocktails, therefore I find it was reasonable that only details of the assault were provided when they were asked "what is the basis of your fear?" Though the Appellants have family members in Canada and possessed information about the refugee process prior to coming to Canada, there is no evidence on record about those family members' level of education, whether they provided any advice to the Appellants regarding what to say at the airport on arrival, or the source and depth of the Appellants' information. For the RPD to suggest that the Appellants ought to have provided more information simply because their family members are in Canada and the Appellants had some familiarity with the refugee process in Canada but without any other evidence, is unreasonable.
[20] With regard to the Appellants' supporting documents, I agree with the Appellants that the RPD was microscopic. The medical documents corroborate the Associate Appellant's elbow injury and I have no reason to doubt their authenticity. Further, considering the objective evidence of how police treat Roma individuals, which says Roma individuals fall victim to several forms of poor treatment from the police including ethnic profiling, disproportionate penalties, and brutality,3 I find that it would be unreasonable to expect the Appellants to have produced a police report.
[21] With regard to the Appellants' housing and social assistance matters, I find the RPD erred in its analysis. The Appellants' arguments have merit and their testimony on these matters was credible. The RPD found the Appellants failed to establish they were living in the area affected by the demolition or that they were evicted. However, the Appellants' arguments make it clear that the RPD over-relied on the address information provided at the POE despite the Appellants' low level of education, that the Appellants did establish through their corroborative evidence that they lived in the area they alleged, that the RPD ignored that the Appellants continued to receive social assistance until they left for Canada as they were living with family members, and that the RPD ignored the objective evidence that some Roma individuals who were forcibly evicted did not receive adequate alternative housing or compensation. For these reasons, I find the RPD erred in its analysis and I find the Appellants established their allegations about their housing and social assistance matters on a balance of probabilities.
[22] I find the Principal Appellant testified credibly about his and the Associate Appellant's experiences of discrimination in the areas of housing, employment, healthcare, and daily life. I have considered that testimony, as well as the information contained in the Appellants' Basis of Claim forms, and I find that for most of their lives they have experienced incidents of discrimination which ultimately led them to leave their home country. I am guided by the UNHCR handbook which states that:
54. Differences in the treatment of various groups do indeed exist to a greater or lesser extent in many societies. Persons who receive less favourable treatment as a result of such differences are not necessarily victims of persecution. It is only in certain circumstances that discrimination will amount to persecution. This would be so if measures of discrimination lead to consequences of a substantially prejudicial nature for the person concerned, e.g. serious restrictions on his right to earn his livelihood, his right to practice his religion, or his access to normally available educational facilities.
55. Where measures of discrimination are, in themselves, not of a serious character, they may nevertheless give rise to a reasonable fear of persecution if they produce, in the mind of the person concerned, a feeling of apprehension and insecurity as regards his future existence. Whether or not such measures of discrimination in themselves amount to persecution must be determined in the light of all the circumstances. A claim to fear of persecution will of course be stronger where a person has been the victim of a number of discriminatory measures of this type and where there is thus a cumulative element involved.4
[23] I have considered the documentary evidence regarding the situation for Roma individuals in Hungary. The United States Department of State report notes that human rights Non-Governmental Organizations continued to report that Roma individuals suffer social and economic exclusion and discrimination in almost all fields of life.5 It is also noted in this document that Romani woman can suffer multiple forms of discrimination on the basis of gender, ethnicity, class and that they experienced barriers to equal access in education, healthcare, housing, employment, and justice.
[24] There exists prejudice by the police against the Roma community and ethnic profiling. It is noted that police response to hate crimes is not adequate.6 There is evidence that right-wing paramilitary groups organize demonstrations against Roma individuals, Jews, and homosexuals.7 Racist violence against Roma individuals remains one of the most pressing issues in the country. Between January 2009 and September 2013 there were more than 100 cases of alleged hate-motivated violence targeting Roma individuals and/or their property. Roma individuals are often subjected to hate speech. These problems have been sustained by the continued hostility Roma individuals face from police forces and officials, reflected in persistent discriminatory practices including ethnic profiling and fines for even the most minor infractions. This also extends to a failure to protect Roma community members from attack.8
[25] Roma individuals in Hungary face discrimination in all fields of life including education, housing, employment, health care, and political participation. The Hungarian Ombudsman has reported that the disadvantageous social situation of Roma individuals is aggravated by discrimination in all of these fields. A report from the Hungarian Central Statistics Office cites that the employment rate for Roma individuals was only 25.9 percent in 2014, as compared to the employment rate for non-Roma individuals, which was 60 percent. This report also states that 67.9 percent of Roma individuals were at risk of poverty. It is noted that 500,000 to 600,000 Roma individuals live in disadvantaged regions of Hungary, mostly in segregated residential zones in poor conditions.9
[26] It is reported that there is an increasing rate of evictions against disadvantaged people and a decreasing amount of social housing. Roma individuals often face discrimination in accessing social housing and municipalities use a variety of techniques to prevent Roma individuals from living in more desirable urban neighbourhoods.10
[27] With regard to health care, there is information that Roma women often lack access to medical services due to direct discrimination and degrading treatment at the hospital. It is reported that Roma women are being segregated in certain medical facilities.11
[28] The documentary evidence references efforts to integrate Roma individuals into the general population. However, there is little information on how the government initiatives have benefited Roma individuals specifically.12
[29] I have considered the documentary evidence concerning the situation for Roma individuals in Hungary relating to discrimination and targeting by right-wing groups, as well as the personal experiences of the Appellants in regard to employment, housing, healthcare, and social exclusion. I find, based on the past experiences of the Appellants and the country conditions evidence, that the Appellants would experience discrimination which would cumulatively lead to consequences of a substantially prejudicial nature that would amount to persecution should they return to Hungary.
[30] I further find that the documentary evidence does not indicate that the police are providing adequate state protection with regard to crimes against Roma individuals. I therefore find that there is not adequate state protection available to the Appellants in Hungary. I further find that there is no Internal Flight Alternative available to the Appellants in Hungary, as the situation for Roma individuals is consistent throughout the country.
CONCLUSION
[31] The RAD finds that there is a serious possibility that the Appellants would suffer persecution should they return to Hungary because of their Roma ethnicity. For this reason, the RAD cannot uphold the decision of the RPD.
[32] The appeal is allowed. Pursuant to section 111(1)(b) of the Immigration and Refugee Protection Act (IRPA), the RAD sets aside the determination of the RPD and substitutes its own determination that the Appellants are Convention refugees.
(signed)
"I. Singh"
I. Singh
March 4, 2020
Date
1 Immigration and Refugee Protection Act, S.C. 2001, c. 27, as amended.
2 Rozas del Solar v. Canada (Citizenship and Immigration), 2018 FC 1145 (CanLII).
3 National Documentation Package (NDP) for Hungary (30 September 2019), items 13.6 and 13.9.
4 Handbook and Guidelines on Procedures and Criteria for Determining Refugee Status under the 1951 Convention and the 1967 Protocol Relating to the Status of Refugees, UNHCR, December 2011.
5 NDP for Hungary (30 September 2019), item 2.1.
6 NDP for Hungary (30 September 2019), item 13.6.
7 NDP for Hungary (30 September 2019), item 13.1.
8 NDP for Hungary (30 September 2019), item 13.11.
9 NDP for Hungary (30 September 2019), item 13.5.
10 Ibid.
